item.title: Where are Australia's EV 'charging deserts'?
item.description: High petrol prices are driving increased demand for EVs. But how easy are they to charge?

item.title: Daylight saving is almost over — how will it affect your time zone?
item.description: When daylight saving ends, Australia effectively loses two of its five time zones.

item.title: Bowen says fuel supply 'strong', tells Easter travellers not to cancel plans
item.description: The energy minister said fuel supplies were holding steady, with dozens of shipments en route and service station shortages declining.

item.title: There were once throngs of pilgrims. Now silence fills Jerusalem's streets
item.description: Easter in Jerusalem has been stilled this year — exposing how Palestinian Christians have long practised faith under constraint.

item.title: 'Even stones tell stories': Inside the historic winery that's survived world wars and a pandemic
item.description: A historic winery preserves tradition while adapting to shifting demand, industry oversupply and an uncertain future.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Paul Griffin tells SBS 'more important than ever' to get flu vaccine this year
item.description: 'Get vaccinated now' was an instruction we heard repeatedly during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic and since then Australia's vaccination rates have been declining. So much so that last year Australia recorded its worst flu season on record with 1,738 deaths. Director of Infectious Diseases at the Mater, Professor Paul Griffin, says everyone should get a flu vaccination. He's been telling SBS that this year's flu vaccine includes protection against a highly contagious mutation of influenza A which is known as subclade K or Super-K.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Are Australia's institutions equipped for gender diversity?
item.description: A national report has found discrimination against transgender people is still widespread across institutions, including in healthcare, housing, education and employment. The report by the Australian Human Rights Commission makes 19 recommendations, including reforms across Australian states and territories to protect gender diverse people from vilification, incitement of hatred and threats of physical harm. The author of the report, Sex Discrimination Commissioner Anna Cody, spoke to Razanne Al-Abdeli about the findings, including identifying the barriers gender-diverse people experience in different institutional settings.

item.title: Economic, fuel-price shocks to be felt long after war in Middle East ends, government warns
item.description: The warning of lingering shocks comes as the NSW and WA governments announced measures to tighten monitoring of fuel retailers.

item.title: This industry faced near total collapse in Australia. How did it come back from the brink?
item.description: Australia's game industry is levelling up, with independent studios hitting high scores despite ongoing challenges.

item.title: Iran responds to Trump's expletive-filled threat; Reports of 45-day ceasefire talks
item.description: US President Donald Trump has reissued an ultimatum to Iran, saying the US will attack its power plants and bridges on Tuesday.

item.title: Iran says World Cup pull-out increasingly likely unless matches relocated
item.description: Iran has confirmed its chances of playing in the US are "very low" as it awaits security guarantees and a decision from FIFA.

item.title: Road deaths continue to rise over Easter long weekend | SBS News in Easy English 6 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Sprint sensation Sha'Carri Richardson sets new record in Stawell Gift
item.description: The Olympic medallist took out the historic 120m handicap race in western Victoria in 13.15 seconds.

item.title: Graham Arnold no longer coaches Australia, but he was mobbed by fans at the airport
item.description: Celebrations span continents as Iraq qualifies amid disruption, with fans gathering in Sydney to thank the coach behind it.

item.title: 'He's the best coach in the world': Ecstatic Iraqi fans greet Graham Arnold
item.description: Former Socceroos coach Graham Arnold has received a rapturous reception at Sydney Airport. Jubilant members of the Iraqi community gathering to greet him upon his return home, after he guided Iraq to their first World Cup berth in 40 years.

item.title: 'Hurting and humiliating us': Iraqis meet the US veterans who shattered their childhoods
item.description: The Iraq War left deep scars for both Iraqi civilians and former US soldiers still coming to terms with their actions.

item.title: 'Need to answer for itself': AG's response after Trump lashes out at NATO and Pacific allies
item.description: The attorney-general has brushed off criticism from Donald Trump, after he shared frustrations over a lack of support from Australia.

item.title: 'Need to answer for itself': AG's response after Trump lashes out at NATO and Pacific allies
item.description: The attorney-general has brushed off criticism from Donald Trump, after he shared frustrations over a lack of support from Australia.

item.title: 'Need to answer for itself': AG's response after Trump lashes out at NATO and Pacific allies
item.description: The attorney-general has brushed off criticism from Donald Trump, after he shared frustrations over a lack of support from Australia.

item.title: Australian War Memorial alters Ben Roberts-Smith display after war crime charges
item.description: The Australian War Memorial said the display would remain on show but had been updated to reflect the allegations.

item.title: Australian War Memorial alters Ben Roberts-Smith display after war crime charges
item.description: The Australian War Memorial said the display would remain on show but had been updated to reflect the allegations.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 7 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Mother-of-three faces deportation after decade-old visa mistake
item.description: A local South Australian community is rallying around for a woman who could be deported to Taiwan in less than a fortnight, leaving behind her husband and 3 young children.Ying-Hsi Chou is pleading with Immigration Minister Tony Burke to allow her to stay in Murray Bridge, after being told she breached her visa over a decade ago.

item.title: EV sales hit a record as ASX climbs to a four month high
item.description: Listen to Australian and world news, and follow trending topics with SBS News Podcasts. TRANSCRIPT: The ASX climbs 1.7 per cent to a four-month high as strong gains sweep across all sectors, with insights from Kai Chen of MPC Markets on what is driving the rally. At the same time, rising fuel prices are helping push electric vehicle sales to new levels, with Tony Weber from the Federal Chamber of Automotive Industries unpacking whether Australia is nearing a turning point. Plus, Kim Farrant from HESTA warns super fund members about the risks of switching investment strategies during market volatility, as more Australians move towards defensive options despite recent market rebounds.

item.title: 'You need to be a breadwinner': The parenting views dividing young dads
item.description: As traditional views of masculinity regain traction among some young men, Kelvin is challenging ideas around a father's role.

item.title: Why Trump can't walk away from the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The US president's inconsistent messaging about the Strait of Hormuz signals no "clear strategy", experts say.

item.title: 'A whole civilisation will die': Trump threatens Iran once more as oil deadline looms
item.description: Iran has rejected Trump's demands and threatened to retaliate against infrastructure belonging to US allies in the Gulf.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz to re-open under ceasefire | SBS News in Easy English 8 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith to remain in custody after arrest over war crimes charges
item.description: The 47-year-old is accused of the murder of unarmed civilians while deployed in Afghanistan between 2009 and 2012.

item.title: Artemis II's journey around the moon: as it happened in pictures
item.description: Here are some of the most amazing images to emerge from the first crewed lunar fly-by in more than 50 years.

item.title: Markets surge as ceasefire hopes spark $80bn rally on the ASX
item.description: Australia’s sharemarket has recorded its strongest session in about a year, with the ASX200 jumping 2.6 per cent and adding around $80 billion in value as investors responded to a reported two-week ceasefire agreement between the United States and Iran. Gains were broad-based, with eight of eleven sectors higher and technology stocks leading the charge, while gold miners and airlines also rallied amid sharp moves in commodity prices. The Australian dollar climbed to around 70.7 US cents. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Kyle Rodda, Senior Financial Market Analyst at Capital.com, to unpack what’s driving the surge and what it could mean for markets next.

item.title: If your stomach feels more sensitive than usual, this could be the reason
item.description: Social eating and exposure to sunlight might be linked to flare-ups of bowel symptoms.

item.title: How Australia's investigations into alleged misconduct in Afghanistan differ to the world's
item.description: Experts say Australia's response to alleged war crimes in Afghanistan has differed from some of its key allies.

item.title: South Australian mother faces deportation after passport mistake
item.description: A South Australian community is rallying around a woman who could be deported, leaving behind her husband and children.

item.title: As a 'clog wog', Australians didn't know I was a migrant, so they confided their racism
item.description: Annette Jorgensen moved here from Denmark aged two. Living as a migrant while looking like a white Australian was an insightful experience.

item.title: Deal or no deal? What is happening with the Iran US ceasefire
item.description: Just hours after the world welcomed news of a two-week ceasefire between the U-S and Iran - which would have seen the Strait of Hormuz open up passage of much of the globe's oil supply - the fragile truce appears to be at serious risk. Iran now says it will target any ships that attempt to cross the major shipping passage - blaming deadly Israeli attacks on Lebanon overnight, which the U-S says were not subject to the ceasefire pact. So what happens now? And what have Iran and the US actually agreed to?

item.title: PM says Middle East ceasefire should include Lebanon | SBS News in Easy English 9 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Record-setting Tropical Cyclone Maila heads for flood-weary Australian region
item.description: Residents of far north Queensland are still cleaning up after Tropical Cyclone Narelle carved through the region just weeks ago.

item.title: 'We cannot face him': Family of Afghan man allegedly killed by Ben Roberts-Smith speaks out
item.description: An Afghan man whose brother and father were allegedly murdered by Australian soldiers has spoken out following Ben Roberts-Smith's arrest.

item.title: US-Iran truce tested, BRS war crime charges & NASA boldly goes…
item.description: A shaky ceasefire announced between US President Donald Trump and Iran as oil prices plummet in response. Decorated veteran Ben Roberts-Smith charged with war crimes, what they could mean for Australia’s military reputation. Plus, allegations of foreign interference overshadow Hungary’s upcoming election, and around the moon and back with Artemis II.

item.title: ASX climbs as fuel habits shift and workers fall behind on AI skills
item.description: The ASX pushes higher after a strong rally, edging closer to record territory, while fresh data reveals Australians are changing how they buy fuel as prices stay elevated. NAB Senior Economist Taylor Nugent unpacks why motorists are topping up more often, and what it says about cost pressures. At the same time, new figures show fewer people are investing in work-related training, raising questions about whether employers are doing enough to prepare workers for an AI-driven future. PwC Chief People Officer Karen Lonergan weighs in on the growing skills gap, before Nick Schoenmaker from Portfolio Construction Forum breaks down the latest moves on the sharemarket and what’s driving investor confidence.

item.title: Commute-cutting proposal for Australian workers, as fuel crisis bites
item.description: Some workers are spending more than $100 extra a week in fuel costs, and there are now calls for more flexible working arrangements.

item.title: He's got Trump and Putin's praise — but are they about to lose their ally in Europe?
item.description: Hungary's election this weekend is "one of the most momentous elections in Europe and for Europe in many years," according to one analyst.

item.title: Israel agrees to Lebanon peace talks as strike deaths climb above 300
item.description: The news comes a day after Israel launched its largest wave of strikes on Lebanon since 2 March.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz traffic still a trickle despite ceasefire, no new attacks
item.description: Hundreds of ships with thousands of crew remain stranded on either side of the Strait.

item.title: What Albanese's trip to Singapore could mean for Australia's fuel supply
item.description: Albanese has warned that a lasting end to the war in the Middle East would not necessarily mean it's "back to business as usual".

item.title: Russia and Ukraine agree to two-day Orthodox Easter ceasefire
item.description: "People need an Easter without threats and a real move towards peace," Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said.

item.title: Discussions on fuel agreement between Australia and Singapore | SBS News in Easy English 10 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Is the Strait of Hormuz actually open? The possible rise of Iran's new world shipping order
item.description: The major shipping waterway has been at the heart of ceasefire discussions, with mixed reporting over its functionality.

item.title: Singapore says it won't restrict fuel to Australia as the countries sign new energy deal
item.description: Australia's largest supplier of refined fuel will continue to provide petrol and diesel amid global uncertainty as part of a new agreement.

item.title: ASX has best week since October 2022 even as super returns slide
item.description: A volatile stretch for markets has taken a bite out of Australians’ super, with SuperRatings CEO Kirby Rappell explaining how recent gains have been erased amid global uncertainty and falling oil prices. The ASX edged lower on the day but closed out its strongest week since October 2022, with mixed sector performance and sharp losses in tech stocks. Oreana Private Wealth Chief Investment Officer Isaac Poole breaks down what is driving the market moves and what investors should watch next, in this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast.

item.title: Why experts seriously doubt the US' claim of 'overwhelming victory' in the war against Iran
item.description: "Everyone loses in this war," one foreign policy analyst told SBS News.

item.title: INTERVIEW: The Cocos Islands will soon become uninhabitable; but what happens to the residents?
item.description: A remote Australian outpost more than 3,000 kilometres from Perth, the Cocos (Keeling) Islands are facing an uncertain future. Climate modelling predicts the low-lying atoll, home to around 600 residents, could become uninhabitable within 50 years. About 460 of those residents are Cocos Malays, whose ancestors were brought to the islands generations ago as indentured labourers by the Clunies-Ross family, paid in company-issued tokens. Despite that history, the community has preserved a distinct culture and way of life that endures today. Following a United Nations-supervised vote in the 1980s, the Cocos Malays chose to integrate with Australia. Since that time, it is the isolation that has helped preserve the islands unique cultural identity. In this extended edition of Weekend One on One, Federal Minister for Local Government and Territories Kristy McBain speaks with SBS’s Christopher Tan, following the release of the Government’s Coastal Hazard Risk Management and Adaptation Plan — the final report assessing the threats facing the Cocos Islands.

item.title: Digital detox: welcome to the offline club for those wanting a screen-free connection
item.description: A new kind of club has emerged in Amsterdam - one where participants choose to go offline, away from their phones and other devices. The gathering reflects a wider trend, especially among younger people, to step away from screens and connect with others and themselves.

item.title: Iran and US still at odds on truce terms as negotiating teams arrive in Islamabad
item.description: Iran says that to end the war in the Middle East, its assets must be unblocked and a ceasefire implemented in Lebanon.

item.title: 'Splashdown confirmed' as Artemis II astronauts return to Earth from record-breaking trip to the moon
item.description: The crew of NASA's Artemis II mission have returned to Earth after creating history on their pass behind the moon. They splashed down in the Pacific this morning, after flying deeper into space than any humans before them.

item.title: Why Pakistan is hosting the highest-level US-Iran talks in almost half a century
item.description: Officials from the US and Iran are set to meet in Islamabad, with Pakistan hoping to broker an end to the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Some Australians forced to choose 'between food and medicine', new survey finds
item.description: A new survey has shed light on the financial decisions faced by some Australians prescribed medicines not covered by the PBS.

item.title: Some Australians forced to choose 'between food and medicine', new survey finds
item.description: A new survey has shed light on the financial decisions faced by some Australians prescribed medicines not covered by the PBS.

item.title: 'A simpler way of being': The rise of the offline club
item.description: There's a growing movement of young people coming together to switch off their phones and rediscover life offline.

item.title: 'Beaten' and 'subject to mock executions': How protesters are being forgotten in war
item.description: Several Iranians have been executed in recent weeks in Iran; some say the regime is sending the "most horrific warning signal".

item.title: No deal: JD Vance leaves Pakistan after US and Iran fail to reach peace agreement
item.description: Foreign Minister Penny Wong said it was "disappointing" that a deal had not been reached, urging the US and Iran to return to negotiations.

item.title: Darwin multicultural walking group connecting migrants one step at a time
item.description: A local program in Darwin is helping refugees and new migrants connect with their community, while building life-long friendships. The walking group may be simple, but organisers say at a time when more people are facing conflict and displacement, the initiative provides a welcome escape.

item.title: Human rights groups warn about systematic persecution of Baha'i followers
item.description: Executions have continued despite war in Iran, with human rights organisations warning the regime is sending a brutal message to citizens who challenge it.Fears are mounting in the minority Baha'i community, with reports some members have been subjected to extreme torture in recent weeks. This story was produced in collaboration with SBS Persian and includes disturbing concepts.

item.title: Why Melbourne woman Ildi wants to see the end of Viktor Orbán's time ruling over Hungary
item.description: From fencers to shopkeepers, Hungarians weigh in on an election that could end Viktor Orbán's 16-year rule.

item.title: More Australians are self-prescribing natural remedies, but are they always safe?
item.description: Widely used in cold and flu supplements, andrographis has been linked to life-threatening allergic reactions.

item.title: Government considering further economic relief as motorists urged to reduce fuel use
item.description: Transport Minister Catherine King has warned that even if the Strait of Hormuz opens soon, there's "a long tail" to the fuel crisis.

item.title: Better than Bolt: Gout Gout smashes 20-second barrier in 200m sprint, beats own record
item.description: The 18-year-old has become the first Australian to legally break the 20-second barrier over 200m after he recorded a time of 19.67.

item.title: Yu went by a different name to seem more Australian, but it backfired
item.description: More than half of Australia's residents or their parents were born overseas, but many still find having a non-English-sounding name hard.

item.title: Inside Australia's push to build a sovereign missile industry
item.description: As Australia ramps up missile production, experts say supply chain gaps and export challenges could limit its independence.

item.title: PM 'disappointed' US-Iran talks failed, says Australia not asked to block Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran has restricted traffic through the strait while allowing vessels deemed to be working for friendly countries to pass.

item.title: 'Europe's heart is beating stronger': Hungary's Orbán concedes landmark defeat
item.description: European Union leader Ursula von der Leyen hailed the landslide victory by the Hungarian opposition as a move towards Europe.

item.title: India grieves as legendary Bollywood singer Asha Bhosle dies age 92
item.description: Asha Bhosle, who recorded more than 12,000 songs in multiple Indian languages over a seven‑decade career, has died in Mumbai.

item.title: 'Selfless, courageous' heroes of Bondi Junction stabbing honoured
item.description: Two years after a mass stabbing at a Sydney shopping centre, eight individuals have been recognised for their bravery.

item.title: 'A dangerous precedent': Iran wants to impose tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. Can it?
item.description: Charging ongoing fees for transit through the Strait of Hormuz would likely trigger international uproar.

item.title: What your dreams could be telling you about your Alzheimer's risk
item.description: Frequent nightmares, or the inability to recall your dreams at all, could be early signs of Alzheimer's disease.

item.title: India begins count of its 1.4 billion people
item.description: India is about to conduct the world’s largest census of its predicted 1.4 billion population after a five year COVID-initiated delay, budgeted for almost two billion Australian dollars.More than 3 million paid surveyors, including school teachers and government officials, will head out and knock on each door in India’s hundreds of thousands villages and cities, as the task unfolds with questions around process, data security and the government claim that caste will be included for the first time since the 1930s.

item.title: Donald Trump calls Pope Leo 'weak' after pontiff's criticism of war and immigration policies
item.description: The United States president told reporters that he is "not a big fan" of Pope Leo XIV.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Defence Industry Minister Pat Conroy on plans for a missile industry
item.description: Australia is stepping into a new phase of its military evolution, producing a type of high-precision, sniper-like missile on home soil for the first time. Along with the United States, it is now one of the only nations manufacturing these guided weapons. Defence Industry Minister Pat Conroy tells Cameron Carr why the federal government believes a sovereign missile industry is so important.

item.title: Who is Péter Magyar, Hungary's new prime minister?
item.description: Railing against the Orbán regime helped his popularity explode. Now, he may be a problem for Trump and Putin.

item.title: United States to blockade Iran's ports
item.description: Marathon talks between the United States and Iran have failed to reach a deal to end the war, jeopardising a fragile two-week ceasefire. The collapse of peace negotiations, and U-S plans to blockade the Strait of Hormuz, have rattled an already-tense region and raised fears of renewed fighting.

item.title: Bondi stabbing heroes honoured with bravery awards | SBS News in Easy English 13 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Blockading the Strait: How Donald Trump's latest strategy could cost Australians
item.description: The US leader has threatened to escalate the war on Iran with a naval blockade that experts warn could be economically disastrous.

item.title: Markets slip as oil surges and investors ask can AI interpret geopolitical risk?
item.description: The ASX edges lower as oil prices climb above US$100 amid escalating Middle East tensions, raising fresh concerns about inflation and growth. Armina Rosenberg from Minotaur Capital breaks down whether AI-driven investing can make sense of geopolitical shocks, while Deloitte Access Economics partner David Rumbens warns Australia could be edging closer to recession, even if the conflict eases. With markets still near record highs but volatility building, this episode unpacks the risks shaping the economic outlook and what it means for investors and households.

item.title: Eight people recognised for Bondi bravery
item.description: Eight people have been recognised for their bravery during the Bondi Junction shopping centre attack. Governor-General Sam Mostyn announced the special honours to mark the two-year anniversary.

item.title: Viktor Orbán has been defeated. What does it mean for the rest of the world?
item.description: Viktor Orbán had such an outsized global influence that his crushing electoral defeat is resounding far and wide.

item.title: House prices are falling in some places, but one state keeps charging ahead
item.description: A property expert said houses in this capital city are selling at "crazy" prices.

item.title: Do young men 'want kids like kids want a puppy'? The findings on gen Z's 'cluck gap'
item.description: Research shows gen Z men are keener (or 'cluckier') on having children than women of the same generation. What's going on?

item.title: Iran remains defiant on Hormuz blockade, Israel and Lebanon prepare for peace talks
item.description: US President Donald Trump said United States forces would block any ships that had paid a toll for passage through the strait.

item.title: Israel to focus on Hezbollah disarmament in Lebanon talks
One Australian city is running out of landfill space, so its trash may head to these rural towns
item.description: Sydney's landfill capacity could run out by 2030, so the city's waste will be incinerated in regional towns — and locals aren't happy.

item.title: Roblox launches accounts for children and teens as grooming concerns grow
item.description: The new accounts come months after the Australian government put the video game giant on notice over numerous child grooming reports.

item.title: Coalition's migration plan panned as 'Trumpian', likened to 'White Australia' era
item.description: Labor, the Greens and rights groups have lined up to condemn Angus Taylor's first major policy proposal as Opposition leader.

item.title: What are Prince Harry and Meghan doing in Australia?
item.description: Despite bearing all the hallmarks of a royal tour, this is not an official visit.

item.title: US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz begins
item.description: A US naval blockade on all maritime traffic through Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz has come into effect, after a deadline passed for a ceasefire deal and reopening of the passage. US President Donald Trump says any Iranian ships that challenge the blockade will be sunk, as Iran blames excessive demands from the U-S for stalling a resolution to the conflict.

item.title: Qantas and Virgin react to global fuel supply shortages — but one is less concerned
item.description: Qantas also says that demand for services to Europe is growing, as travellers avoid Middle East routes.

item.title: Donald Trump refuses to apologise for strong criticism of Pope Leo | SBS News in Easy English 14 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: It's all change for Hungary after Viktor Orbán was ousted
item.description: Hungarian voters continue to celebrate after ousting Prime Minister Viktor Orbán after 16 years in power. The election result was a rejection of his authoritarian, far-right agenda in favour of pro-European challenger Peter Magyar. But for the new leader, the coming months won't be easy.

item.title: Uber riders to pay fuel surcharge as prices surge
item.description: Australian consumers will be paying a temporary fuel surcharge for their Uber trips if they choose to ride in a petrol or hybrid car. The new measure will begin from Wednesday, and end on June 8. It comes at a time when the transport sector is introducing fee adjustments to cope with the ongoing fuel crisis.

item.title: Man charged with using newly banned phrases in Queensland tries to argue law 'insane'
item.description: A 70-year-old activist has made a unique plea against charges of publicly reciting a prohibited expression during a protest.

item.title: From Jesus imagery to a 'holy war': Does Donald Trump really think he's divine?
item.description: There has been fierce backlash to Donald Trump's depiction of himself as a Jesus-like figure.

item.title: Qantas cancels flights as war hits corporate Australia's earnings
item.description: The ASX edges higher despite fresh warnings from corporate Australia, with Qantas and Westpac flagging the financial impact of the Middle East conflict as cost pressures build. Ricardo Gonçalves unpacks the latest market moves and what they mean for inflation and interest rates, speaking with NAB Chief Economist Sally Auld and LGT Wealth Management CIO Scott Haslem, while new data reveals rental stress has hit record levels. Cotality’s Head of Research Gerard Burg joins to explain why Australians are now spending more than ever on housing and what’s driving the shortage.

item.title: Strong backlash as Angus Taylor unveils Coalition’s controversial immigration policy
item.description: Opposition leader Angus Taylor has outlined the Coalition's new hardline immigration policy. The plan has drawn widespread criticism and strong condemnation from critics, including Labor and human rights groups.

item.title: Australians are the biggest cocaine users. But they don't know 'how many lives' are in a gram
item.description: The journey of cocaine from Colombia to Australia is stained with blood. But Colombia's president is trying to forge a new path.

item.title: Construction industry calls for 'once in a generation' reform ahead of housing crisis talks
item.description: Builders are calling for changes to the National Construction Code amid soaring overheads as regulatory costs bite.

item.title: What's at stake as Israel and Lebanon hold first talks in more than three decades
item.description: Lebanon and Israel have begun rare direct talks in Washington DC, but deep divisions and ongoing violence could stall any deal.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Singapore Energy Minister Dr Tan See Leng
item.description: After Prime Minister Albanese’s whistle-stop visit to Singapore, the future of Australia’s fuel supplies looks a lot more secure as the ripples from the Straits of Hormuz spread outwards. Last week Anthony Albanese met with his Singaporean counterpart Lawrence Wong in Singapore. The two signed off on a new bilateral trade agreement, under which Australia would guarantee Singapore’s supply of liquefied natural gas, and Singapore would prioritise the flow of refined petroleum to Australia. Singapore is Australia’s largest source of refined petrol. SBS's Naveen Razik secured an exclusive interview with Singapore's Energy Minister Dr. Tan See Leng about our energy partnership and the wider consequences of the current crisis in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Zelenskyy says US has 'no time for Ukraine' as Iran peace focus keeps negotiators busy
item.description: Zelenskyy pointed to the issue of deliveries of US arms to Ukraine, saying it had become "a big problem".

item.title: 'Really dangerous': Chalmers reacts to IMF's recession warning as he heads to US
item.description: The treasurer said Australia was "better placed" than many countries but would not be immune from the economic fallout.

item.title: 'Couldn't do it in Britain': Why Meghan and Harry's Australia tour is an 'experiment'
item.description: The four-day visit could be mistaken for a royal tour, but is actually a private trip considered more commercial in nature.

item.title: Damning ombudsman's report finds disabled brothers suffered after health department failures
item.description: The brothers, who were admitted to hospital and treated for severe malnutrition, are now in state care.

item.title: Speculation that US Iran peace talks could resume | SBS News in Easy English 15 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Tony Burke slams Coalition for 'politicising' flight of Palestinians from Gaza
Australia marks 35 years since final report of Royal Commission into Aboriginal Deaths in Custody
Millions uprooted, a country 'disintegrated': World's largest displacement crisis deepens
item.description: As Sudan's civil war enters its fourth year, fears are growing some may be forced into "devastating coping strategies" amid aid shortfalls.

item.title: Pastoral and political - the Pope visits Africa
item.description: Pope Leo the Fourteenth is on a historic tour of Africa, a journey spanning four countries, thousands of kilometres, and a continent that is now central to the future of the Catholic Church. From Algeria to Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea, the visit is being described as both pastoral and political.

item.title: How Trump's latest Iran war moves could bring China out of the shadows
item.description: Chinese President Xi Jinping made a rare public comment on the war in the Middle East.

item.title: 'Would be a different country': Burke defends migration as Taylor policy criticism mounts
item.description: The government has rejected the recently announced Coalition plans to deport migrants who breach "Australian values".

item.title: Airfares rise as Japan overtakes Indonesia as Australian favourite and ASX rises
item.description: Airfares are set to climb as Virgin Australia cuts flights to manage rising fuel costs, while Qantas flags a sharp increase in its fuel bill, reshaping travel plans for Australians. Phoebe Deas speaks with Ellis Taylor from Cirium on what these changes mean for the aviation sector, as new data reveals Japan has overtaken Indonesia as Australia’s second most popular destination, helped by a stronger exchange rate. Meanwhile, the ASX closes slightly higher, with insight from Scott Phillips of The Motley Fool on the market’s mixed performance.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Vanuatu's minister for Climate Change Ralph Regenvanu
item.description: Vanuatu’s Minister for Climate Change, Ralph Regenvanu, has emerged as one of the Pacific’s most influential and principled public figures, combining political leadership with cultural advocacy and a sustained commitment to climate justice. In an exclusive interview with SBS, he said that current issues with fuel security and supply wouldn’t influence the negotiations between Australia and Vanuatu to finalise the Nakamal agreement. The treaty, initialed in August last year but not finalised, aims to enhance economic, security and cultural cooperation between the two countries. He told SBS’s Rayane Tamer that the current fuel crisis presents Australia with a unique opportunity to become an alternative energy superpower

item.title: The groceries that could rise 20 per cent in price, as farmers issue warning
item.description: Australian farmers are warning Australians will be hit by food price increases due to ongoing diesel and fertiliser supply constraints.

item.title: 'New nuclear arms race': Marles says Australia faces most threatening climate since WWII
item.description: A renewed strategy for the Australian Defence Force will provide billions of dollars more for projects over the coming decade.

item.title: The lessons from the Middle East war Australia can't afford to ignore
item.description: Global conflict is exposing Australia's vulnerabilities in energy, trade and defence — testing the nation's preparedness.

item.title: I changed my surname when I was 14. It ended up being a lifeline
item.description: When Jess O'Halloran's family unit broke down, so did a part of her identity.

item.title: Australia's military set for record peacetime spend | Morning News Bulletin 16 April 2026
item.description: Australia set for a record boost to military spending; The U-S looking to impose more financial sanctions on countries and companies who support Iran; The Matildas beat Kenya in Nairobi.

item.title: 'This is a setback': Bowen outlines impact of oil refinery blaze as investigation begins
item.description: A Viva Energy spokesperson said the company would conduct a "thorough investigation" into the incident.

item.title: 'This is a setback': Bowen outlines impact of oil refinery blaze as investigation begins
item.description: A Viva Energy spokesperson said the company would conduct a "thorough investigation" into the incident.

item.title: Trump says war 'very close to being over' as US flags fresh talks with Iran
item.description: The US president said negotiations with Iran could resume in the coming days after earlier talks failed to result in a deal.

item.title: 'Name the Beast': Report warns racism rising across Australia
item.description: A new Human Rights Commission report highlights the devastating impacts of discrimination against communities in Australia - targetting Jewish, Muslim, Palestinian, Arab and Israeli communities - since the October 7, 2023 attacks. The federal government is facing urgent calls to implement the National Anti-Racism Framework - which they've had access to for almost two years - to combat racism.

item.title: What we know so far about the oil refinery fire in Geelong
item.description: An investigation is underway to determine the cause of the fire, but it is not being as suspicious. Here's what we know so far.

item.title: Trump says Iran war could 'end soon' - as new Israeli strikes hit Lebanon
item.description: U-S President Donald Trump continues to talk up prospects for an end to the war in the Middle East. In media interviews, he has told the world to watch out for an amazing two days ahead of new ceasefire talks, while U-S forces continue their blockade on vessels leaving Iranian ports. But Israel's campaign in Lebanon is casting doubt on prospects for peace.

item.title: Geelong oil refinery ‘significantly damaged’ after ferocious fire | Midday News Bulletin 16 April 2026
item.description: A major fire to limit fuel production at one of Australia's last two oil refineries; Harry and Meghan meet with fans in Melbourne on their not so royal tour; FIFA says Iran will play in the World Cup this year.

item.title: US pilot Daniel Duggan set to be extradited from Australia after failed appeal
item.description: A federal court judge has dismissed former US Marine pilot Daniel Duggan's extradition appeal.

item.title: Israeli strikes kill four medics, targeting two rescue teams, Lebanese authorities say
item.description: Lebanese authorities accused the Israeli military of deliberately targeting rescue workers.

item.title: Fire at fuel refinery in Geelong | | SBS News in Easy English 16 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Import nation: How Australia went from eight oil refineries to two
item.description: Australia only has two oil refineries and one caught fire overnight, igniting further concerns about the nation's fuel security.

item.title: Minns says NSW government 'disappointed' as protest laws ruled unconstitutional
item.description: A judge in NSW's top court has struck down the state's laws regarding restrictions on protests.

item.title: Evening News Bulletin 16 April 2026 | Australia Secures Additional Diesel Supplies
item.description: Australia secures 100 million litres of additional diesel imports; Richard Marles announces a $14 billion increase in defence spending; Shepparton is set to host two rounds of the BMX Racing World Cup.

item.title: How Australia's defence spend compares globally, as Marles' numbers questioned
item.description: The government's newly announced 2026 National Defence Strategy will see the budget grow by $53 billion over the next decade.

item.title: Trump’s Hormuz gambit, Hungary ousts Orbán & Colombia’s cocaine war
item.description: US-Iran talks collapse, and China feels the pressure over President Trump’s blockade in the Strait of Hormuz. Plus, a stunning election victory signals a new era for Hungary and how Australia is helping to fuel Colombia’s drug trade.

item.title: ASX falls despite Wall Street record as jobless rate holds and AI reshapes workplace
item.description: The ASX eases despite strong leads from Wall Street, while Australia’s unemployment rate holds at 4.3 per cent and companies grapple with rising costs. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with HSBC Chief Economist Paul Bloxham on what the latest labour market data means for interest rates, and with David Lane from Ord Minnett on the day’s market moves. Plus, Mark Thirlwell from the Australian Institute of Company Directors explains why artificial intelligence and productivity pressures are keeping business leaders awake at night.

item.title: Creative accounting or record investments? Labor unveils 2026 National Defence Strategy
item.description: The Australian Government has unveiled the 2026 National Defence Strategy, outlining reprioritised investments in drones, missile defence and undersea warfare capabilities. While the government claims the new strategy includes record defence spending allocations, the opposition has accused Labor of artificially inflating the numbers.

item.title: How to get the best view of the Lyrid meteor shower in Australian skies
item.description: Stargazers can rejoice as another celestial event passes through Earth's atmosphere.

item.title: At least 16 in Ukraine reportedly killed as Russia launches 2026's deadliest attack so far
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said the attack proved that Russia didn't deserve any easing of sanctions.

item.title: Australia's neighbour criticises Albanese and will 'definitely' ask China for fuel help
item.description: Vanuatu has urged Australia to confront its reliance on fossil fuels, while not ruling out seeking support from Canberra.

item.title: The 'crop' we're not harvesting: Why Australia's brightest source is still underused
item.description: As solar farms spread across regional Australia, some farmers are cashing in while others say the rollout is too fast.

item.title: 'Uncharted territory': How the war in the Middle East is testing the FIFA World Cup
item.description: Iran's participation in the World Cup is looking uncertain, posing a complex logistical and geopolitical challenge for FIFA.

item.title: Hours after ceasefire with Israel, a lifeline to south Lebanon reopens
item.description: Thousands of civilians made their way south to their homes after a ceasefire went into effect overnight.

item.title: Israel and Lebanon agree to a 10-day ceasefire | Morning News Bulletin 17 April 2026
item.description: Israel and Lebanon agree to a 10-day ceasefire and will hold further talks; Court rules laws restricting protests in New South Wales, unconstitutional; Queensland stands by plan to use crocodile habitat for rowing events at the 2032 Olympics.

item.title: Pope Leo slams 'tyrants' as Trump says the pontiff must understand 'nasty world'
item.description: US President Donald Trump says it's important for Pope Leo to understand Iran is a global threat, having called him "weak" on foreign policy.

item.title: 'Endless grief' as Türkiye mourns young victims of school shooting
item.description: While Türkiye has endured earthquakes, mining disasters and terror attacks, it had not experienced a mass killing at a school.

item.title: Calls to drop demonstrators' charges as NSW anti-protest laws ruled unconstitutional
item.description: The court's decision could prompt prosecutors to withdraw charges and pave the way for others to sue.

item.title: PM signals no fuel restrictions after refinery blaze and faces questions on Trump's tirade
item.description: The US president has said that he is "not happy" with Australia over the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Albanese warns of fuel supply impacts after Geelong refinery fire | Midday News Bulletin 17 April 2026
item.description: A 10-day truce between Israel and Lebanon goes into effect; Anthony Albanese warns of fuel supply impacts after Geelong refinery fire; Former Arsenal goalkeeper Alex Manninger killed in car crash, aged 48.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith released from prison after securing bail over alleged war crimes
item.description: The 47-year-old is accused of the murder of unarmed civilians while deployed in Afghanistan between 2009 and 2012.

item.title: Iranian footballers thank Australia for 'safe haven' but ask for privacy
item.description: Two Iranian players have expressed their gratitude for being offered a safe haven in Australia after the Asian Cup tournament.

item.title: Ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon | SBS News in Easy English 17 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Fakes, missile cruisers, unusual encounters: A front-row seat to the global energy crisis
item.description: The aftershocks of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz can be seen with the naked eye at one of the world's busiest ports.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith granted bail| Evening News Bulletin 17 April 2026
item.description: Ben Roberts-Smith granted bail; opposition says Anthony Albanese has not done enough to secure fuel shipments; Hannah Green is the best-placed Australian at the L-P-G-A tour event.

item.title: 10-day ceasefire agreement reached between Israel and Lebanon
item.description: Israel has agreed a 10-day ceasefire with the Lebanese government off the back of recent talks in Washington. Hezbollah has not been directly included in negotiations but appears willing to uphold the temporary truce. US President Donald Trump says leader-level talks are expected to follow in coming days.

item.title: Struggling to find a job, Victor makes a living by crying for strangers
item.description: Mourners for hire can help families offer a "better send-off" in communities where funerals are steeped in culture.

item.title: Paid to Cry: Kenya’s Professional Mourners in Demand
item.description: How do you guarantee a good turn out at a funeral? After all, the last thing you want to happen as you pass on to whatever lies beyond the grave... is to go without at least a bit of fuss. In Kenya, some families are hiring professional mourners to give their loved ones the ultimate send-off.

item.title: ASX ends three week winning streak despite another record on Wall Street
item.description: The ASX200 has edged lower, snapping a three-week run despite fresh record highs on Wall Street, with weakness in consumer discretionary stocks offset by gains in tech and buy now, pay later players. In this short end-of-week update, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Dianne Colledge from Morgans Financial Limited about what is driving the local market, the outlook for key sectors and how global momentum is shaping sentiment at home, alongside moves in the Australian dollar and oil prices.

item.title: Australia joins international forum on reopening the Strait of Hormuz | Morning News Bulletin 18 April 2026
item.description: Australia joins an international forum on reopening the Strait of Hormuz; Southern Lebanon residents returning home as a ceasefire comes into effect;Gout Gout prepares for a key race at the Australian Athletics Junior Championships.

item.title: 'Borrowing from the past': This old way of living is making a comeback in Australia
item.description: My grandparents' home is my home too, and that's becoming more common in Australia.

item.title: With more rate hikes looming, here's what borrowers should do
item.description: As economists warn of more interest rate hikes by Christmas, some borrowers are wondering if it's time to refinance their mortgage.

item.title: Iran threatens to re-close Strait of Hormuz if US doesn't withdraw blockade
item.description: United States President Donald Trump has said the US' blockade would remain until "our transaction with Iran is 100% complete".

item.title: Iran threatens to re-close Strait of Hormuz if US doesn't withdraw blockade
item.description: United States President Donald Trump has said the US' blockade would remain until "our transaction with Iran is 100% complete".

item.title: Simmering tensions: Tripe soup row boils over
item.description: A beloved dish shared by both Turkish and Greek people has sparked new cultural tensions between the two countries.A Greek restaurant owner is striving to register tripe soup with the United Nations' cultural body, UNESCO, but the move has sparked uproar in neighbouring Turkiye.

item.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz again, as Trump warns Tehran not to 'blackmail' the US
item.description: The US had broken a promise by continuing its naval blockade of ships sailing to and from Iranian ports, Iran's military said.

item.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz again, as Trump warns Tehran not to 'blackmail' the US
item.description: The US had broken a promise by continuing its naval blockade of ships sailing to and from Iranian ports, Iran's military said.

item.title: Albanese welcomes reopening of Strait of Hormuz but says arrangement still 'very fragile'
item.description: The prime minister also said that "Australia stands ready to support efforts to restore stability and security" in the strait.

item.title: 'It impacted the relationship with my culture': When culture, cuisine and a chronic illness combine
item.description: Coeliac disease affects one in seventy Australians, according to the Wesley Research Institute. But when food is integral to cultural identity, the impacts of this autoimmune disease — especially on multicultural and diasporic communities in Australia — can extend beyond the physical, affecting mental health. Jude Soussan is a young person of Lebanese heritage in Perth, Western Australia, and has used her diagnosis to help educate others about what it means to live with coeliac disease in the diaspora, using creativity. She hopes that, with time, communities can better understand how to accommodate chronic illness.

item.title: President Trump vows US blockade will continue until a deal is reached with Iran
The 'real tightrope' Australia will have to walk to ease inflation and cut oil dependence
item.description: Experts say the fuel crunch and war in the Middle East present an opportunity to reduce Australia's dependence on imported fuels.

item.title: What Gallipoli can teach us about today's war in the Middle East
item.description: The war in the Middle East reminds how easily small strategic moves can mushroom into expanding military commitments.

item.title: While the world's eyes were on the moon, Australia quietly axed a major space deal
item.description: The end of a significant membership has some wondering whether Australia's days as an astronomy powerhouse are numbered.

item.title: Australia, world shipping warily welcomes Hormuz Strait opening | Midday News Bulletin 18 April 2026
item.description: Major shipping companies and world leaders wary as the Strait of Hormuz reopens to commercial traffic; No extra fuel security measures after a Geelong oil refinery inferno; Carlos Alcaraz out of the upcoming Madrid Open.

item.title: European leaders continue plans to open Hormuz | Evening News Bulletin 18 April 2026
item.description: ** The UK and France say plans continue for an international maritime security mission in the Strait of Hormuz... Energy Minister Bowen says fuel shortages are easing, and extends temporary relaxation of sulfur limits ... and in sport, eighteen-year-old sprint star Gout Gout claims the National Juniors 100 metre gold.

item.title: European leaders plan to open Hormuz
Fuel shortages ease says Bowen
UNICEF workers killed in Gaza
Gout Gout claims 100 metre gold
Protests draw attention to gendered violence
Twenty protesters arrested for violating Queensland's ban on pro-Palestinian phrases
item.description: Those who recite or display "from the river to the sea" or "globalise the intifada" in Queensland can face up to two years' jail.

item.title: 'A stable life': The matchmaker setting up North Korean women with South Korean men
item.description: Yujin runs a matchmaking agency — and her clients are North Korean women and South Korean men looking to date.

item.title: 'Damn it, let's go after him': What the Democrats need to topple Donald Trump
item.description: The midterm elections are on the horizon, and the contest to succeed Donald Trump is quietly underway.

item.title: Iran again closes the Strait of Hormuz | Morning News Bulletin 19 April 2026
item.description: The Strait of Hormuz closed again a day after Iran agreed to passage during the Israel ceasefire; A pedestrian killed outside a Melbourne comic convention; Adelaide win by a single point in a thriller against St Kilda.

item.title: All change for the FIFA World Cup 2026
item.description: In just a few weeks, the world’s football fans will be turning all their attention on North America, as the highly anticipated FIFA World Cup finally gets underway. But some may find it’s not entirely what they were expecting.

item.title: Macron blames Hezbollah as French peacekeeper killed, three wounded in Lebanon
item.description: Hezbollah has denied any involvement in the attack, expressing 'surprise at positions that rushed to make baseless accusations.'

item.title: The Strait of Hormuz closes again
'Situation is dire': No progress amid 35th anniversary of deaths in custody report
item.description: Since the report's release, more than 630 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people have died in the custody of police or corrective services.

item.title: Australians cut back on stockpiling petrol, reduce fuel spend as households adjust
item.description: New analysis from a major bank shows Australians have cut their spending on fuel and are going back to filling up when they need it, rather than panic buying.

item.title: Man arrested over death of pedestrian at Melbourne’s Comic Con | Midday News Bulletin 19 April 2026
item.description: A man arrested over the death of a pedestrian outside a Melbourne comic convention; Pakistan locks down the capital in preparation for more ceasefire talks; The Melbourne Vixens reach the top of the Super Netball ladder.

item.title: The simple medication switch that could save Australians over $165 million
item.description: Among other changes, a basic software update could save many Australian patients both time and money.

item.title: Between two homes: Migrant workers in Lebanon roped into social and economic impact of war
item.description: Migrant workers in Lebanon are one of the most vulnerable communities since the war in the Middle East erupted. Experts say they are trapped between the economic impacts of the war, and it's taking a toll on their sense of belonging.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith vows to fight all charges, criticises police over 'sensational arrest'
item.description: Accused of murdering five unarmed Afghan detainees, Ben Roberts-Smith has used his first public comments since his arrest to say he is proud of his service in Afghanistan and will fight the charges against him.

item.title: Deputy PM says matter of global urgency to reopen Strait of Hormuz | Evening News Bulletin 19 April 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, Ben Roberts-Smith says he will use his trial to clear his name over alleged war crimes; Deputy PM Marles says it's a matter of global urgency to reopen Strait of Hormuz; and in sport, Manchester City prepare for do or die show-down with Arsenal.

item.title: 'One thing after another': The almost $20,000 penalty for being single in Australia
item.description: Being single comes at a "premium" in Australia, leaving those not in a relationship thousands of dollars worse off.

item.title: Over 60 per cent of popular Cheaper Home Batteries Program installations 'substandard'
item.description: A new regulatory report says the installation problems it found risk not only the credibility of the scheme, but also public safety.
